narrative_blocks.header: Secretary of the Treasury under Eisenhower, 1953-1957
narrative_blocks.body: George Magoffin Humphrey served as the 55th Secretary of the Treasury under President Eisenhower from January 21, 1953 to July 29, 1957 — his signature appearing on approximately 4.5 billion currency bills circulating worldwide — managing federal finances in the post-Korean War economy and advocating so forcefully for balanced budgets that Eisenhower was quoted as saying, 'When George speaks, we all listen.' Before entering government, he had built the M.A. Hanna Company from a $2 million deficit to profitability as president (1929-1952), and after leaving the Cabinet he led post-WWII Allied reparations negotiations in Germany, served as chairman of National Steel Corporation, and remained a central figure in Republican economic policy.

narrative_blocks.header: Named Magoffin for his grandmother's Hooker family line
narrative_blocks.body: George Magoffin Humphrey's middle name traces directly to his grandmother's family: his mother was Caroline Mary Magoffin, whose father was Reverend James Hooker Magoffin, whose mother was Mary Chaffee Hooker of Windsor, Connecticut — making 'Hooker' a given name in the Magoffin family for three consecutive generations before it became 'Magoffin' in a Secretary of the Treasury. The genealogical thread runs unbroken back through Irondequoit, New York and Schenectady to the Hooker family of colonial Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: Gave up $300,000 to serve as Eisenhower's treasurer
narrative_blocks.body: Humphrey gave up a $300,000 annual salary as president of M.A. Hanna Company to accept the Cabinet position at $22,500 — a sacrifice that underscored his conviction about fiscal conservatism — and in a 1957 press conference warned that if federal spending was not curbed, 'you will have a depression that will curl your hair.' He left office July 29, 1957, returning to private industry as honorary board chairman of Hanna and later chairman of National Steel Corporation, and his papers (11 linear feet, MS3132) are held at the Western Reserve Historical Society in Cleveland.

narrative_blocks.header: Led Allied dismantling of the Nazi war machine
narrative_blocks.body: After World War II, President Truman appointed George Magoffin Humphrey to lead the Reparations Survey Committee advising Allied countries on dismantling the German war industry — a role that positioned him as a de facto international industrial statesman, overseeing the controlled destruction of the same kind of industrial infrastructure he had spent decades building and managing at M.A. Hanna. It was this assignment that brought him to the attention of General Lucius D. Clay, who recommended him to Eisenhower for the Treasury secretaryship — meaning the reparations work in Germany, not his decades in Cleveland, was the event that placed him in Eisenhower's Cabinet.

quotes.text: When George speaks, we all listen.
You will have a depression that will curl your hair.
quotes.attribution: George M. Humphrey, 1957 press conference, on unchecked federal spending

sources.label: Encyclopedia of Cleveland History — George Magoffin Humphrey
Western Reserve Historical Society — George Magoffin Humphrey Papers MS3132

narrative_blocks.header: His father built Hanna; he became its chairman
narrative_blocks.body: Gilbert Watts Humphrey followed his father George Magoffin Humphrey as chairman of the M.A. Hanna Company — the Cleveland-based mining and industrial conglomerate his father had taken from a two-million-dollar deficit to profitability as president, then left for Eisenhower's Cabinet in 1953 — sustaining a family corporate dynasty that straddled two generations of American industrial capitalism. He died in 1979, the year his father's estate disposed of 278,560 shares of Hanna Mining Company common stock through a First Boston Corporation underwriting group to settle estate and tax obligations, closing out the family's decades-long grip on the company.

burial.plot_notes: Forest Lawn Cemetery Saginaw per FA — buried in Michigan despite dying in Florida

narrative_blocks.header: Graduated Helmuth College and attended University of Michigan
narrative_blocks.body: Caroline Mary Magoffin Humphrey graduated from Helmuth College in London, Ontario, Canada — an Anglican women's college — before attending the University of Michigan, an unusual educational path for a woman born in Portage, Wisconsin in 1861, at a time when fewer than two percent of American women attended college at all. She married Watts Sherman Humphrey, a Civil War veteran and frontier attorney, in Chicago in January 1888, and their eldest son George Magoffin Humphrey became Eisenhower's Secretary of the Treasury — suggesting that her educational ambition was not lost on the generation she raised.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

bio.photo_notes: Watts Sherman Humphrey Sr. (1844-1916), Civil War veteran and Cheboygan attorney

notable.notable_blurb: Civil War cavalryman, Cheboygan attorney, and director of

education.notes: Studied law under Kilbourne in Lansing before graduating.

career.notes: Corporate counsel for Michigan Sugar and railroads; founded firm after arriving Cheboygan 1869.

career.organization: Mistee Lumber; Phelps Dodge Corporation; mining and smelting interests
career.notes: Significant corporate directorships in industrial Michigan.

military_service.notes: Enlisted December 11, 1863; discharged May 18, 1865. Horse shot under him at Battle of the Wilderness (May 1864); wounded at Battle of Trevilian Station (June 1864). Admitted Harper's Hospital Detroit January 2, 1865.

institutions.institution_blurb: Graduated University of Michigan Law 1869; went on to found Humphrey & Perks law firm in Cheboygan, Michigan.

narrative_blocks.header: Horse shot at the Wilderness; wounded at Trevilian
narrative_blocks.body: Watts Sherman Humphrey served as a private in Company I, 1st Michigan Cavalry, enlisting December 11, 1863, and saw fierce action: his horse was shot under him at the Battle of the Wilderness in May 1864, and he was wounded at the Battle of Trevilian Station in June 1864 — two of the bloodiest cavalry engagements of the Overland Campaign — before being admitted to Harper's Hospital in Detroit on January 2, 1865. He was honorably discharged May 18, 1865, and afterward studied law under Kilbourne in Lansing before graduating from the University of Michigan in 1869.

narrative_blocks.header: Founded a Cheboygan law firm; ran for Congress
narrative_blocks.body: Watts Sherman Humphrey arrived in Cheboygan, Michigan in 1869 with his University of Michigan law degree and built one of the region's most prominent practices, founding Humphrey & Perks and serving as corporate counsel to Michigan Sugar and railroad interests; he was elected county treasurer in 1880 and in 1890 ran as the Republican candidate for Congress. His corporate reach extended to directorships at Mistee Lumber and Phelps Dodge — one of the nation's largest copper mining companies — making him a significant figure in the industrial development of northern Michigan.

narrative_blocks.header: A corporate lawyer for Michigan's mines and railroads
narrative_blocks.body: From his Cheboygan law practice, Watts Sherman Humphrey built his corporate reach to become a director of Phelps Dodge Corporation — then one of the largest copper mining companies in the United States, with mines in Arizona and New Mexico — and of Mistee Lumber, wiring together the extractive industrial economy of northern Michigan through legal work for railroads and mining interests that made him a significant figure in the region's development. His connections to natural resources and corporate law directly presaged his son George Magoffin Humphrey's career as president of M.A. Hanna Company and ultimately Secretary of the Treasury — two generations of men who ran the industrial sinew of American capitalism before entering public life.

sources.label: Cheboygan History article — The Humphrey Brothers
Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

bio.title: Reverend
bio.photo_notes: Reverend James Hooker Magoffin (1820-1883), Episcopal priest

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Hooker' honors his mother Mary Chaffee Hooker's maiden name — daughter of Captain James Hooker of Windsor, CT.

narrative_blocks.header: Named Hooker to honor his mother's maiden name
narrative_blocks.body: Reverend James Hooker Magoffin carried 'Hooker' as his middle name to honor his mother Mary Chaffee Hooker, and passed the tradition forward — his son Frank was named 'Frank Hooker Magoffin,' making three consecutive generations of the Magoffin family to carry 'Hooker' as a given name. His grandson George Magoffin Humphrey became the 55th Secretary of the Treasury under President Eisenhower, carrying the heritage in the form of the middle name 'Magoffin.'

narrative_blocks.header: An Episcopal priest in a Presbyterian father's household
narrative_blocks.body: Reverend James Hooker Magoffin was ordained as an Episcopal priest despite being raised in a Presbyterian household — his father had been an elder in the Presbyterian Church in Schenectady — making him one of the quiet denominational crossovers of mid-nineteenth-century American religion, a son who took his faith in a different direction while preserving his mother's Hooker name in every way he could. He moved west from New York through Wisconsin, where his son Frank Hooker Magoffin was born in Waukesha in 1859, to Cheboygan County, Michigan, dying there in 1883 at sixty-three — following the American frontier even as an Episcopal clergyman.

burial.plot_notes: Forest Hill Cemetery Ann Arbor per FA

career.notes: Born in the town of Benton per FA. Served in Michigan area.

INST064.notes: Douglas Woodruff Hillman (HD2502): B.A. 1946, LL.B. 1948 (Law School). Also served as lecturer at the Institute for Continuing Legal Education, Ann Arbor, 1979-1987. | Merged from INST131: Ann Arbor Michigan. Two documented Hooker-tree connections: I00953 Watts Sherman Humphrey Sr. (LL.B. 1869; graduated same year Cheboygan article confirms alongside brother George Plumer Humphrey) and HD4679 George Magoffin Humphrey (LL.B. 1912; 'received his LL.B degree from the University of Michigan in 1912' per Case Western Encyclopedia). Both subsequently practiced law.
